does: This paper introduces a comprehensive safety evaluation benchmark for Vision Language Models (VLLMs) that goes beyond standard performance metrics to assess out-of-distribution generalization and adversarial robustness. The authors present two novel VQA datasets with variants designed to test model behavior under challenging conditions, propose a straightforward attack strategy to mislead VLLMs into producing unrelated responses, and evaluate two jailbreaking approaches targeting either the vision or language components. Testing 21 models including open-source variants and GPT-4V, they reveal that current VLLMs struggle with out-of-distribution text but not images unless visual information is limited, and that models can be easily misled through vision encoder attacks despite their multimodal training, suggesting that vision-language alignment often compromises safety protocols.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research examining safety and robustness in multimodal AI systems, extending prior work on adversarial attacks and out-of-distribution generalization from pure language models and computer vision to the intersection of both modalities. It responds to emerging concerns about Vision LLMs as they become deployed at scale, drawing on jailbreaking literature that has explored methods for circumventing safety mechanisms in language models, while introducing novel evaluation paradigms specific to the vision-language setting. The paper engages with existing VQA benchmarks and their limitations, addressing how models that perform well on standard visual reasoning tasks may still fail on safety-critical measures when faced with distribution shifts or coordinated attacks.
enables: This benchmark enables future work in developing more robust and genuinely aligned Vision LLMs by providing systematic evaluation methodology for vision-language safety. It points toward designing better training procedures that do not sacrifice safety for multimodal performance, investigating stronger defenses against vision-encoder attacks, and improving out-of-distribution robustness in the text modality. The findings suggest that future research should focus on disentangling vision-language interactions during safety training, developing better alignment techniques that preserve safety properties across both modalities, and creating stronger evaluation suites that capture real-world failure modes in deployed systems.
